section.title: Edom's Violence Against Jacob
section.sectionRationale: Here the oracle states the charge behind the sentence. On the day Jerusalem fell, Edom did not stand up for its brother Jacob; it stood aloof, then gloated, then joined the looting and even hunted down the fugitives. Six times the prophet hammers the phrase the day of their distress, exposing the treachery of a brother.
section.thesis: Edom's crime was the betrayal of a brother in his worst hour: standing by, cheering the ruin, seizing the plunder, and cutting off the escaping survivors, so its own shame and cutting off are just.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the summary charge of violence against brother Jacob, covered with shame; and the drumbeat of what Edom did on the day, aloof, gloating, looting, and blocking the fugitives.

units.label: The charge: violence against a brother
units.note: covered with shame, cut off forever

verses.text: Because of the {{chamas|violence}} against your {{ach|brother}} Jacob, you will be covered with shame and cut off forever.
On the {{yom|day}} you stood aloof while strangers carried off his wealth and foreigners entered his gate and cast lots for Jerusalem, you were just like one of them.
But you should not gloat in that day, your brother’s day of misfortune, nor rejoice over the people of Judah in the day of their destruction, nor boast proudly in the day of their distress.
You should not enter the gate of My people in the day of their disaster, nor gloat over their affliction in the day of their disaster, nor loot their wealth in the day of their disaster.
Nor should you stand at the crossroads to cut off their fugitives, nor deliver up their survivors in the day of their distress.

chamas.gloss: Violence, wrong, cruel injustice. It is the same word that filled the earth before the flood, Genesis 6:11, and describes bloodshed and oppression against the helpless. Edom's chamas against its brother is the specific crime for which judgment is announced.

ach.gloss: Brother. The word insists on the family bond between Edom and Israel, the sons of the twin brothers Esau and Jacob. To call the victim your brother Jacob turns Edom's crime from mere enmity into betrayal of kin, deepening its guilt.

yom.gloss: Day. Obadiah repeats this word like a hammer blow through verses 11 to 14, the day of misfortune, destruction, distress, and disaster. The relentless drumbeat fixes attention on that one terrible day when Jerusalem fell and Edom chose the wrong side, and it sets up the greater Day of the LORD in verse 15.

basic.context: This is the heart of the accusation. When Jerusalem was attacked and fell, Edom did not help its relatives; it stood back and watched, then celebrated, then joined in the looting, and even blocked the roads to catch those trying to escape. Because Edom did violence to its brother, God says it will be covered with shame and cut off.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage lists real actions Edom took on the day of Jerusalem's fall, most likely in 586 BC. The prophet repeats the day again and again to drive home the horror of kicking a brother when he is down. What is condemned here, gloating over another's ruin and profiting from it, is condemned for anyone.
basic.originalLanguages: The crime is chamas, violence, the same word for the cruelty that filled the earth before the flood. The victim is called your brother, ach, which turns enmity into betrayal of family. And the word day, yom, is repeated like a drumbeat, ten times in these verses, fixing the eye on that one terrible day.
basic.typology: Edom betraying its brother in his hour of need is a dark picture of the world's cruelty toward God's suffering people, and even a faint echo of a friend turned traitor, answered ultimately at the cross where Christ was handed over.
basic.christ: Christ is the true brother who, unlike Edom, does not stand aloof from His people in distress but enters into their suffering to rescue them. Where Edom handed over the survivors, Jesus lays down His life so that not one of His own is lost.
basic.god: We learn that God notices how we treat others in their worst moments, especially the vulnerable. He takes the side of the afflicted, calling Judah My people, and He holds the bystander who gloats as guilty as the invader.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 137 remembers Edom crying tear it down over Jerusalem. Ezekiel 35 and Amos 1 also indict Edom for its cruelty. Proverbs 24:17 warns against rejoicing when your enemy falls.
basic.oneStory: Edom's betrayal of its brother is the crime at the centre of Obadiah. It shows the world's hostility to God's people at its ugliest, and it sets up the reversal to come: the God who sees the wrong will repay it and deliver His own.

context.literary: These verses give the ground for the judgment announced in verses 1 to 9. The prophet moves from a summary charge in verse 10, violence against your brother Jacob, to a vivid, escalating catalogue in verses 11 to 14: standing aloof, gloating, boasting, entering the gate, looting, and finally hunting fugitives. The repeated refrain the day of their distress, sounded again and again, gives the passage the rhythm of an accusation read aloud.
context.historical: The scene fits the fall of Jerusalem to Babylon in 586 BC, when the city's gates were breached, its wealth carried off, and lots were cast over the spoil. Edom, sharing a border with Judah, is remembered elsewhere for urging on the destruction and for seizing fleeing Judeans at the mountain passes, then occupying southern Judah. Such treachery by a kindred neighbour was felt as a peculiarly bitter wound.
context.canonical: Edom's conduct on that day is remembered across Scripture: Psalm 137:7 recalls the Edomites shouting to raze Jerusalem to its foundations, and Ezekiel 35 and Lamentations 4:21-22 press the same charge. This makes Edom the archetype of the gloating enemy, and its judgment a promise that God will vindicate His people against those who rejoice in their downfall.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the specific guilt of Edom, the betrayal of a brother in his day of ruin, and to justify the sentence of shame and cutting off, so that God's people know their sufferings are seen and their betrayers will answer for it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes and condemns Edom's actions; the string of you should not clauses is not a set of standing commands but an indictment of what Edom in fact did. The abiding truths are that gloating over another's calamity is sin, that betrayal of kin is grievous, and that God repays such wrong.

debates.question: Are the ‘you should not’ clauses commands, warnings, or an indictment of past acts?

views.label: A retrospective indictment
views.case: Read against the fall already described, the clauses catalogue what Edom actually did, gloating, looting, blocking escape; the negative form vividly relives the crime rather than forbidding a future one.

views.label: Prohibitions Edom violated
views.case: The plain form is do not, so some read them as the standing obligations of a brother that Edom trampled, its guilt lying in doing the very things it should never have done.

debates.bearing: Either reading lands in the same place: Edom is guilty of these very acts, and the passage teaches both what a brother owes in the day of distress and what God will judge when it is withheld.

study.originalLanguages: The section is built on three words. The charge in verse 10 is chamas, violence, the brutal, bloody wrong that once filled the earth before the flood; here it is aimed at kin. That kinship is pressed by ach, brother, and by the name Jacob: this is not a stranger but the twin's descendant. Then the prophet takes up yom, day, and will not let it go, the day you stood aloof, your brother's day, the day of their destruction, distress, disaster; the relentless repetition makes the reader stand again in that single ruinous day and watch Edom choose treachery, and it prepares the ear for the greater Day of the LORD announced next.

typology.type: The brother who betrays in the day of distress
typology.fulfillment: The world's, and even a friend's, betrayal of the righteous sufferer, answered at the cross where Christ was handed over yet saved His own.
The enmity that would cut off God's people, overturned by Christ the true brother who ensures none of His own are lost, John 6:39.

study.christ: Edom is the anti-brother: it stands aloof from Jacob's agony, then profits from it, then blocks the roads of escape. Jesus is the opposite in every line. He is the brother who is not ashamed to call His people brothers, Hebrews 2:11-12; He does not stand aloof from their distress but shares their flesh and blood to help them; and where Edom handed over the survivors to the sword, Christ hands Himself over so that of all His Father gave Him He loses none. The gloating enemy earns shame and cutting off; the faithful brother bears shame and is cut off from the land of the living, Isaiah 53:8, to save the very people Edom would destroy.
study.god: The LORD sees exactly how the vulnerable are treated in their worst hour, and He counts the bystander who gloats as guilty as the invader who plunders. He is not neutral: He calls Judah My people and takes their suffering personally. He is just, matching Edom's crime with its due, shame for its scorn and cutting off for its cutting off of fugitives. And He is faithful, so that betrayal of His people never has the last word.

crossReferences.note: Remember, O LORD, what the Edomites did on the day Jerusalem fell, when they cried, tear it down to its foundations.
Edom is charged with harbouring ancient hostility and handing the Israelites over to the sword in their time of calamity.
Edom pursued his brother with the sword and stifled all compassion, the same charge of fraternal violence.
Do not gloat when your enemy falls; the LORD may turn His wrath from him, a wisdom Edom trampled.

study.oneStory: Edom's betrayal of its brother is the crime at the centre of Obadiah and a portrait of the world's cruelty toward God's people at its ugliest. Yet it sets up the great reversal of the whole story: the God who saw the wrong done on that terrible day will repay it on the Day of the LORD, and He will send a true brother, Christ, who enters His people's distress to save rather than to plunder.

sermon.why: No sermon on Obadiah 1:10-14 from a trusted expositor could be confirmed by web search in this session, so none is listed.

quiz.q: What is the specific crime for which Edom is condemned in this passage?

options.text: Violence against its brother Jacob on the day of Jerusalem's ruin.
options.note: Verse 10 names the violence against your brother Jacob as the ground of judgment.

options.text: Worshipping idols instead of the LORD.
options.note: Idolatry is condemned elsewhere, but here the charge is violence against a brother.

options.text: Failing to pay tribute to Judah.
options.note: No tribute is in view; the crime is betrayal of a brother in distress.

options.text: Refusing to trade with Jerusalem.
options.note: The charge is violence and treachery, not a trade dispute.

quiz.q: How did Edom act on the day Jerusalem fell?

options.text: It stood aloof, gloated, joined the looting, and cut off those trying to escape.
options.note: Verses 11 to 14 trace this escalation from bystander to active betrayer.

options.text: It sent soldiers to defend Jerusalem's gates.
options.note: The opposite: Edom entered the gate to loot, not to defend.

options.text: It quietly mourned but did nothing.
options.note: It did far worse than passive mourning; it gloated and plundered.

options.text: It sheltered the refugees who escaped the city.
options.note: It blocked and handed over the fugitives rather than sheltering them.

quiz.q: Why does the prophet keep repeating the phrase ‘the day of their distress’?

options.text: To fix attention on that one terrible day and the horror of betraying a brother in it.
options.note: The drumbeat of day, yom, drives home the weight of Edom's treachery.

options.text: To mark the many different days over many years.
options.note: It centres on one climactic day, not a spread of separate days.

options.text: Simply as a poetic filler with no real force.
options.note: The repetition is deliberate and heavy with meaning, not filler.

options.text: To predict the exact date of Edom's own fall.
options.note: It recalls Judah's day of ruin; it does not date Edom's fall.

quiz.q: What does calling the victim ‘your brother Jacob’ add to the charge?

options.text: It turns the crime from enmity into betrayal of kin, deepening Edom's guilt.
options.note: Edom and Israel descend from the twins Esau and Jacob, so this is treachery against family.

options.text: It simply gives a geographical location.
options.note: Jacob names the kindred people, not a place.

options.text: It shows the two nations had no relationship at all.
options.note: The point is the opposite: they are brothers, which makes it worse.

options.text: It softens the charge by appealing to family loyalty.
options.note: It does not soften the charge; the broken family bond makes it graver.

quiz.q: How does Christ stand in contrast to Edom in this passage?

options.text: He is the true brother who enters His people's distress to save them rather than standing aloof.
options.note: Hebrews 2 shows Christ unashamed to call us brothers, sharing our suffering to help us.

options.text: He, like Edom, keeps His distance until the danger passes.
options.note: The opposite is true: Christ draws near in suffering, unlike Edom.

options.text: He simply repeats Edom's judgment on all nations.
options.note: Christ does bring judgment, but the contrast here is His brotherly rescue.

options.text: He has no relationship to this passage at all.
options.note: Christ is the faithful brother the passage's failure points toward.

questions: Edom stood aloof while its brother suffered. Where might God be calling you to step in for someone in distress instead of watching from a safe distance?
The passage condemns gloating over another's downfall. Is there anyone whose troubles you have secretly enjoyed, and what would repentance look like there?
Edom profited from a brother's ruin. Are there ways you are tempted to gain from others' losses, and how does the gospel reshape that?
God called the sufferers My people and took their pain personally. How does it comfort you to know that God notices exactly how you are treated in your worst moments?
Christ is the brother who draws near in our distress rather than standing aloof. How can His nearness change the way you draw near to others who are hurting?

translationNotes.note: Cut off forever renders a phrase of decisive, lasting judgment; historically Edom did cease to exist as a distinct nation, its people displaced and absorbed by later centuries.
Cast lots for Jerusalem pictures the victors dividing the spoil and captives by lot; you were just like one of them equates the gloating bystander with the plundering foreigner.
Nor boast proudly renders a phrase the BSB footnote gives literally as nor enlarge your mouth, that is, do not speak arrogantly or gloat with open mouth over another's ruin.
The repeated you should not clauses, read against the fall already described, function as a vivid indictment of what Edom actually did, not merely as warnings for the future.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Obadiah 1:10-14. The BSB footnote at verse 12 rendering nor enlarge your mouth is reflected in the translation notes. No em dashes occur in this section.
